elements.sent-2411-001: Investigation of the generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions containing two parameters has been a very active research area over the last decade.
elements.sent-2411-002: We believe, however, that their monotonicity and convexity properties with respect to parameters have not been thoroughly studied.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we make an attempt to fill this gap.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our results are not complete; for some functions, we manage to establish (log)-convexity/concavity in parameters, while for others, we only managed the prove monotonicity, in which case we present necessary and sufficient conditions for convexity/concavity.
elements.sent-2411-005: In the course of the investigation, we found two hypergeometric representations for the generalized cosine and hyperbolic cosine functions which appear to be new.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the last section of the paper, we present four explicit integral evaluations of combinations of generalized trigonometric/hyperbolic functions in terms of hypergeometric functions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Keywords: generalized trigonometric functions; generalized hyperbolic functions; $(p,q)$-Laplacian; log-convexity; log-concavity; integral representation; hypergeometric function MSC: 33E30; 33E20; 26D07; 33C05.
elements.sent-2411-008: The class of functions nowadays known as the generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions can be traced back to the 1879 paper of Lundberg \cite{Lundberg (1879) .}
elements.sent-2411-009: They were then rediscovered many times, typically in connection with the study of the “generalized circle” $|x|^p+|y|^p=1$ or as eigenfunctions of one-dimensional $p$-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-010: Details can be found in the book \cite{Lang-book (2016) by Lang and Edmunds.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Earlier history has been described by Peetre and Lindquist; see \cite{Lindqvist(1995), Lindqvist(2003) .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The recently published textbook \cite{Poodiack(2022) makes the topic accessible to students.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The generalized trigonometric functions containing two parameters appeared in the works of \^{O tani \cite{Otani(1984) and Dr\'{a bek and Man\'{a sevich \cite{Drabek(1999) in connection with $(p,q)$-Laplacian.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Namely, these authors defined a two-parameter generalized sine function $\sin_{p,q (y)$ in \cite{Drabek(1999) as the inverse function to the integral \begin{equation}\label{eq:arcsinpq} y=\arcsin_{p,q}(x)=\int_0^x\frac{dt}{(1-t^q)^{1/p}}=x{_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!\begin{array}{c}1/p,1/q 1+1/q\end{array}\right|x^q\!\right),\end{equation} with $p,q>1$, $x\in[0,1]$ and $y\in[0,\pi_{p,q /2]$, where \begin{equation}\frac{\pi_{p,q}}{2}=\int_0^1\frac{dt}{(1-t^q)^{1/p}}=\arcsin_{p,q}(1)=\frac{1}{q}B(1/q,1/p'),~~p'=p/(p-1).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Here and below, ${ _2F_1$ stands for the Gauss hypergeometric function (\cite{Andrews(1999) , Definition~2.1.5).}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Note that $\pi_{p,q}\downarrow2$ as $q\uparrow\infty$ and as $p\uparrow\infty$ while monotonically decreasing from infinity.
elements.sent-2411-017: The function $\arcsin_{p,q :[0,1]\to[0,\pi_{p,q /2]$ is an increasing homeomorphism and its inverse $\sin_{p,q (y)$ is well defined.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: We can extend $\sin_{p,q (y)$ to $[\pi_{p,q /2,\pi_{p,q ]$ by $\sin_{p,q (y)=\sin_{p,q (\pi_{p,q -y)$ and further to $[-\pi_{p,q ,0]$ by oddness and to the whole $\mathbb{R}$ by $2\pi_{p,q}$ periodicity (\cite{Edmunds(2012) , (2.4)).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is known that this extension is continuously differentiable on $\R$ and $C^{\infty}$ everywhere except at the points $\{k \pi_{p,q /2; k\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ (\cite{Edmunds(2012) , p.. 49).}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The function $\cos_{p,q (y)$ is then defined naturally by \begin{equation}\label{eq:cos-defined} \cos_{p,q}(y)=\frac{d}{dy}\sin_{p,q}(y).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-021: It is not hard to show using \eqref{eq:arcsinpq that this implies \begin{equation}\label{eq:cossinsum} |\cos_{p,q}(y)|^p+|\sin_{p,q}(y)|^q=1\end{equation} on the whole real line (\cite{Edmunds(2012) , (2.7)) and we can drop the absolute values if we restrict our attention to the interval $[0,\pi_{p,q /2]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Differentiating this identity with respect to $y$ and using \eqref{eq:cos-defined after a little rearrangement, we obtain: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Dcos} \frac{d}{dy}\cos_{p,q}(y)=-\frac{q}{p}\sin_{p,q}^{q-1}(y)\cos_{p,q}^{2-p}(y).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-023: It was observed in \cite{Baricz(2015) that using the change of variable $u=(1-t^q)^{1/p}$ in \begin{equation}\arccos_{p,q}(x)=\arcsin_{p,q}(w)=\int_0^w(1-t^q)^{-1/p}dt,\quadwhere\quad w=(1-x^p)^{1/q},\end{equation} we obtain the representation \begin{equation}\label{eq:arccospq} \arccos_{p,q}(x)=\frac{p}{q}\int_x^1(1-u^p)^{1/q-1}u^{p-2}du,\quad\text{where}\quad 0\leq x\le1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The generalized hyperbolic $(p,q)$-arcsine function is defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:arcsinhpq} \arcsinh_{p,q}(x)\!=\!\!\int_0^x\frac{dt}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}\!=\!x{_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!{c}1/p,1/q 1+1/q\right|\!-\!x^q\!\right) =\bigg(\!\frac{x^p}{1+x^q}\!\bigg)^{\!\!1/p}\!\!\! {_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!{c}1,1/p 1+1/q\right|\frac{x^q}{1+x^q}\!\right),\end{equation} where $p,q>1$, $x\in[0,\infty]$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Denote \begin{equation}\label{eq:hyperpi} \hat{\pi}_{p,q}=2\arcsinh_{p,q}(\infty)= \begin{cases}(2/q)B(1/p-1/q,1/q), p<q \infty, q\le{p}. \end{cases}\end{equation} Note that $\hat{\pi _{p,q}\downarrow2$ as $q\uparrow\infty$ and $\hat{\pi _{p,q}\downarrow\pi_{q =2\pi/(q\sin(\pi/q))$ as $p\downarrow1$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-034: The following hypergeometric representations seem to be new.
elements.sent-2411-035: Indeed, we obtain from \eqref{eq:arccospq using substitution $t=u^p$: \begin{equation}\frac{q}{p}\arccos_{p,q}(x)=\frac{1}{p}\int_0^1(1-t)^{1/q-1}t^{-1/p}dt-\frac{1}{p}\int_0^{x^p}(1-t)^{1/q-1}t^{-1/p}dt =\frac{1}{p}B(1/q,1-1/p)-\frac{x^{p-1}}{p-1} {_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!{c}1-1/p,1-1/q 2-1/p\right|x^p\!\right),\end{equation} where we used the easily verifiable formula (derived by changing the variable in Euler's representation (\cite{Andrews(1999) , Theorem~2.2.1) by direct term-wise integration of the binomial expansion) \begin{equation}\label{eq:betaincomp} \int_{0}^{x}t^{a-1}(1-t)^{b-1}dt=\frac{x^a}{a}{_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!\begin{array}{c}a,1-b a+1\end{array}\right|x\!\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Next, we apply the connection formula (\cite{Andrews(1999) , 2.3.13), which for the values of parameters in question reduces to (after application of the binomial theorem ${ _1F_0(a;-;x)=(1-x)^{-a}$) \begin{equation}{_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!{c}1-1/p,1-1/q 2-1/p\right|x^p\!\right) =x^{1-p}\frac{\Gamma(2-1/p)\Gamma(1/q)}{\Gamma(1+1/q-1/p)}-q\Big(1-\frac{1}{p}\Big)(1-x^p)^{1/q}{_{2}F_{1}}\left(\left.\!\!{c}1,1+1/q-1/p 1+1/q\right|1-x^p\!\right).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-045: Differentiation then yields: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dy}\tam_{p,q}(y)=\frac{\cos_{p,q}^{1+p/q}(y)+\sin_{p,q}^{q}(y)\cos_{p,q}^{1+p/q-p}(y)}{\cos_{p,q}^{2p/q}(y)} =\cos_{p,q}^{1-p/q}(y)(1+\tam_{p,q}^{q}(y))=(1+\tam_{p,q}^{q}(y))^{1/q-1/p+1},\end{equation} where we applied \eqref{eq:tamcos in the last equality (alternatively, one can differentiate \eqref{eq:tamcos directly).}}
elements.sent-2411-046: This differentiation formula was first found in (\cite{Miyakawa(2021) , p.. 16).}
elements.sent-2411-047: Hence, for the inverse function, we obtain the representation \begin{equation}\label{eq:arctamintegral} \arctam_{p,q}(x)=\int_{0}^{x}\frac{dt}{(1+t^q)^{1+1/q-1/p}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: This representation is also seen from the connection formula $\tam_{p,q (y)=\sinh_{r,q (y)$, $r=pq/(pq+p-q)$ (\cite{Miyakawa(2022) , Theorem~2.1).}}}

elements.sent-2411-050: In a similar fashion, (\cite{Miyakawa(2022) , Theorem~2.2) indicates that \begin{equation}\tamh_{p,q}(y)=\sin_{r,q}(y),~~\text{where}~r=pq/(pq+p-q),\end{equation} so that from \eqref{eq:arcsinpq , we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:arctamhintegral} \arctamh_{p,q}(x)=\int_{0}^{x}\frac{dt}{(1-t^q)^{1+1/q-1/p}}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:tamhp} \arctamh_{p',p}(x)=\int_{0}^{x}\frac{dt}{(1-t^p)^{2/p}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The literature on the two-parameter trigonometric and hyperbolic functions has grown rapidly over the last two decades and we will not attempt to survey it in this introduction.
elements.sent-2411-052: The reader is invited to consult the paper \cite{Lindqvist(2004) for a historical overview, the introductory parts of \cite{Karp(2015),Kobayashi(2019),Takeuchi(2019) , the survey \cite{Yin(2019) and the books \cite{Lang-book (2016),Poodiack(2022) for numerous further references to more modern research and connections with various areas of mathematics.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Inequalities for the generalized trigonometric functions with two parameters have been studied in \cite{Baricz(2015),Bhayo(2012) for fixed parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Research on convexity properties with respect to parameters is scarce.
elements.sent-2411-055: A few exceptions include \cite{BBV(2015) , where Tur\'{a n-type inequalities for the inverse trigonometric functions, also those with two parameters, were established, and our paper \cite{Karp(2015) concerned with functions with one parameter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Both are listed in the survey paper (\cite{Yin(2019) , Section~5).}
elements.sent-2411-057: The purpose of this note is two-fold.
elements.sent-2411-058: First, we investigate the monotonicity and convexity properties of the generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions with respect to their parameters $p$ and $q$.
elements.sent-2411-059: We believe that these types of results have not previously appeared in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-060: This is shown in the subsequent Section~\ref{sec2 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Second, we derive several integrals of the inverse and direct functions in terms of hypergeometric functions, which we also believe to be new.
elements.sent-2411-062: These results can be found in Section~\ref{sec3 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, our Lemma~\ref{lm:arccos2F1 above gives new representations for the inverse generalized cosine and hyperbolic cosine functions, which are useful both from the theoretical viewpoint and for an effective computation of these functions and their inverses.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The following lemma will be our key tool for the forgoing investigation of the properties of the generalized trigonometric functions.
elements.sent-2411-065: Its proof is an exercise in calculus; details can be found in (\cite{Karp(2015) , Lemma~1).}

elements.sent-2411-067: Then, the following relations hold true: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial{p}}g(p,y)=-f'_{p}/f'_x,\label{eq:dgdalpha} [5pt] \frac{\partial^2}{\partial{p^2}}g(p,y)=\left(f'_x\right)^{-2} \left\{2f'_{p}f''_{xp}-f'_xf''_{pp}-\left(f'_{p}\right)^2f''_{xx}/f'_x\right\}, \label{eq:d2gd2alpha} [5pt] \frac{\partial^2}{\partial{p^2}}\log[g(p,y)]=(xf'_x)^{-2}\left\{2xf'_{p}f''_{p{x}}-xf'_xf''_{pp}-x(f'_{p})^2f''_{xx}/f'_x-(f'_{p})^2\right\}, \label{eq:d2loggd2alpha}\end{equation} where $x$ on the right is related to $y$ on the left by $y=f(p,x)$ or $x=g(p,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-068: We summarize the monotonicity properties in the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-069: The functions $q\to\sin_{p,q (y)$ and $q\to\tamh_{p,q (y)$ are increasing on $(1,\infty)$ for each fixed $y\in (0,1)$ and $p\in(1,+\infty)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The functions $p\to\cos_{p,q (y)$, $q\to\cos_{p,q (y)$, $q\to\cosh_{p,q (y),$ $q\to\tam_{p,q (y)$ and $q\to\sinh_{p,q (y)$ are, generally speaking, not monotonic (i.e., for each of them, there exist values of $y$ such that no monotonicity takes place).. \end{Theorem Proof.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-080: According to Lemma~\ref{lm:derivatives , to prove that $p\to\cosh_{p,q (x)$ is decreasing, we need to show that \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial{p}}\cosh_{p,q}(y)=-f_{p}'/f_{x}'<0,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}f(x)=\arccosh_{p,q}(x)=\int_0^{(x^p-1)^{1/q}}(1+t^q)^{-1/p}dt.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Differentiation using the Leibniz integral rule yields ($x>1$): \begin{equation}f_{p}'= \frac{1}{p^2}\int_0^{(x^p-1)^{1/q}}(1+t^q)^{-1/p}\log(1+t^q)dt+\frac{x^{p-1} ( x^p-1)^{1/q-1} \log(x)}{q}>0,\end{equation} \begin{equation}f_{x}'=\frac{p x^{ p-2} (x^p-1)^{1/q-1}}{q}>0.\end{equation}. \includegraphics[width=13.7 cm]{Pic4.png { {Figure~1.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The figure shows graphs of the hyperbolic $(p,q)$-arccosine at different values of $q$.
elements.sent-2411-083: By comparing the points of intersection of the graphs with the level lines $y=0.88$ and $y=0.99$, one can notice the absence of monotonicity of the hyperbolic $(p,q)$-cosine as a function of $q$.
elements.sent-2411-084: A positive function $f$ defined on a finite or infinite interval $I$ is said to be logarithmically convex, or log-convex, if its logarithm is convex, or, equivalently, \begin{equation}f(\lambda{x}+(1-\lambda)y)\leq[f(x)]^{\lambda}[f(y)]^{1-\lambda}~~for all~x,y\in{I}~~and~\lambda\in[0,1].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: The function $f$ is log-concave if the above inequality is reversed.
elements.sent-2411-086: If the inequality is strict for $\lambda\in(0,1)$, then the respective property is called strict.
elements.sent-2411-087: It can be seen from the definitions that log-convexity implies convexity and concavity implies log-concavity but not vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-088: In the context of generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions, the function $f(x,p)$ from Lemma~\ref{lm:derivatives is given by an integral of a positive function $\varphi(t,p)$ of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:gen1} y=f(x,p)=\int_{0}^{x}\varphi(t,p)dt\end{equation} which defines $x=g(y,p)$ implicitly for $x\in I$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Then, convexity/concavity of $p\to{g(y,p)}$ is determined by the sign of the~expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:conv1} 2\varphi'_p(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi'_p(t,p)dt-\varphi(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi''_{pp}(t,p)dt -\left(\int_0^x\varphi'_p(t,p)dt\right)^2\varphi'_x(x,p)/\varphi(x,p),\end{equation} while logarithmic convexity/concavity is determined by the sign of \begin{equation}\label{eq:logconv2} 2x\varphi'_p(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi'_p(t,p)dt-x\varphi(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi''_{pp}(t,p)dt- (1+x\varphi'_x(x,p)/\varphi(x,p))\left(\int_0^x\varphi'_p(t,p)dt\right)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-090: In particular, if for all $x\in I$ and $p>1$, the following inequalities hold: \begin{equation}\varphi_x'(x,p)>0,\ D_1(x,p)=(\varphi'_p(x,p))^2-\varphi'_x(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi''_{pp}(t,p)dt<0,\end{equation} then the function $p\to g(p,y)$ is concave on $p>1$.
elements.sent-2411-091: For logarithmic concavity, the following conditions are sufficient: \begin{equation}\varphi_x'(x,p)>0, D_2(x,p)=x(\varphi'_p(x,p))^2-\left(1+x\frac{\varphi'_x(x,p)}{\varphi(x,p)}\right)\varphi(x,p)\int_0^x\varphi''_{pp}(t,p)dt<0.\end{equation}. \end{Lemma Proof.}

elements.sent-2411-117: We need to show that for any $x\in(0,\infty)$ and any $p,q\in(1,\infty)$, \begin{equation}\frac{(q-p)x^q-p}{px(1+x^q)^{1-1/p}}\left(\int_0^x\lambda_1(p,t)dt\right)^2+2\log(1+x^q)\int_0^x\lambda_1(p,t)dt +\int_0^x\lambda_{2}(p,t)dt\ge0,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\lambda_1(p,t)\!=\!\frac{\log(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}, \lambda_2(p,t)\!=\!\frac{(2p-\log(1+t^q))\log(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}=2p\lambda_1(p,t)-\frac{\log^2(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Substituting the second expression for $\lambda_2(p,t)$, writing $\mu(p,x)=\int_0^x\lambda_1(p,t)dt$ and rearranging the first terms, we can rewrite the required inequality as \begin{equation}\frac{qx^{q-1}}{p(1+x^q)^{1-1/p}}\mu(p,x)^2+2(\log(1+x^q)+p)\mu(p,x) \ge\frac{(x^q+1)^{1/p}}{x}\mu(p,x)^2+\int_0^x\frac{\log^2(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}dt,\end{equation} or, dividing by $\mu(p,x)$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:divided} \frac{qx^{q-1}}{p(1+x^q)^{1-1/p}}\mu(p,x)+2(\log(1+x^q)+p) \ge\frac{(x^q+1)^{1/p}}{x}\mu(p,x)+\frac{1}{\mu(p,x)}\int_0^x\frac{\log^2(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-121: According to Lemma~\ref{lm:LHospital , \begin{equation}\frac{r(x)}{s(x)}>\frac{r'(x)}{s'(x)}=\frac{1}{\log(1+x^q)},\end{equation} which is precisely \eqref{eq:srlog .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Hence, if we substitute the rightmost term in \eqref{eq:divided by $\log(1+x^q)$, we increase the right-hand side, so that the new inequality \begin{equation}\frac{qx^{q-1}}{p(1+x^q)^{1-1/p}}\mu(p,x)+2(\log(1+x^q)+p) \ge\frac{(x^q+1)^{1/p}}{x}\mu(p,x)+\log(1+x^q)\end{equation} is stronger than \eqref{eq:divided .}}

elements.sent-2411-127: Differentiating \eqref{eq:form1 with respect to $x$ after substantial simplification, we obtain \begin{equation}f_1'(x)-f_2'(x)=\frac{q^2x^{q-1}}{p(1+x^q)^2}\int_0^x\frac{\log(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}dt+\frac{2p(1+x^q)-qx^q}{(1+x^q)^{1+1/p}},\end{equation} so that $f_1'(x)-f_2'(x)>0$ is equivalent to \begin{equation}\frac{q^2x^{q-1}}{p(1+x^q)^{1-1/p}}\int_0^x\frac{\log(1+t^q)}{(1+t^q)^{1/p}}dt>qx^q-p(1+x^q)-p(1+x^q)\end{equation} which is trivially true in view of \eqref{eq:xpqcondition .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: This proves \eqref{eq:form1 and we are done.. $ \square$.}

elements.sent-2411-154: The proofs for the remaining integrals are similar.. $ \square$.
elements.sent-2411-155: In this paper, we considered the generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions with two parameters.
elements.sent-2411-156: A short survey of their basic properties is presented in the introduction and it includes two presumably new representations for $\arccos_{p,q}$ and $\arccosh_{p,q}$ in terms of the Gauss hypergeometric function.
elements.sent-2411-157: The main body of the paper is concerned with inequalities for the generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions, namely monotonicity and convexity in parameters.
elements.sent-2411-158: We establish monotonicity for all functions where it takes place and (logarithmic) convexity/concavity for $p\to\tamh_{p',p}$ ($p'=p/(p-1)$), $p\to\sin_{p,q}$, $q\to\sin_{p,q}$ and $p\to\sinh_{p,q}$.
elements.sent-2411-159: The final section is devoted to integral evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-160: We derive explicit formulas in terms of the generalized hypergeometric functions for four integrals of inverse and four related integrals of direct generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions.
elements.sent-2411-161: Finally, let us mention some potential applications of the results of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-162: Applications in approximation theory have been considered in \cite{BL2015 , where the basis properties of the generalized sine function with two parameters were examined.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Very recently the generalized trigonometric functions were found to play an important role in constructing explicit solutions of a nonlinear generalization of the Schr\"{o dinger equation.}
elements.sent-2411-164: In particular, they enter the general solution of the traveling wave ansatz, as explained in \cite{GPPCT2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-165: Other potential applications include using generalized trigonometric and hyperbolic functions as activation functions for neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-166: In particular, the classical hyperbolic tangent function has been used in this capacity in neural network models; see \cite{DCH(2022) (Section~3).}
elements.sent-2411-167: Adding two parameters $p$ and $q$ will give an additional degree of flexibility to such models, as they play an entirely different (and highly nonlinear) role from the weights of the model.
elements.sent-2411-168: The work of the second author was supported by the state assignment of the Institute of Applied Mathematics FEBRAS (No.075-00459-24-00).
